segments.s574-g25: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” بشنید is the simple past 3rd-person singular “he heard.” یانس is the subject (proper name). بجوشید is also past 3rd-person singular used figuratively (“he seethed/boiled with anger”); the verb order (subject then verb) is typical of classical Persian narrative. و گفت links the action to his speech: و is the simple conjunction “and,” گفت is the past 3rd-person singular “he said.”
segments.s574-g26: که introduces reported speech or a subordinate clause meaning “that.” کین برادر is a genitive phrase “the hatred/vengeance of a brother” (کین = vengeance, modified by برادر). نشاید is an impersonal/modal present-past-form meaning “it is not fitting/it should not.” نهفت functions as a verbal adjective or verbal noun “be hidden / be kept secret,” so نشاید نهفت = “ought not to be hidden.”
segments.s574-g27: بزد is past 3rd-person singular “he struck/beat.” کوس is the object “war-drum” (no را because poetic/telegraphic style). و is the conjunction “and.” آورد is past 3rd-person singular “he brought.” بیرون is an adverb “out” placed before the noun. صلیب is the direct object “cross.” The sequence (verb—object / verb—adverb—object) reflects flexible, slightly inverted poetic word order.
segments.s574-g28: This is a noun phrase with noun + adjective order common in Persian: صلیب بزرگ = “the great cross.” و joins the next coordinated noun phrase سپاهی مهیب = “a fearsome/terrible army,” where سپاهی is used as a noun (“army”) and مهیب is its postpositive adjective (“awe‑inspiring/terrible”).
segments.s574-g29: سپه را: سپه (army) with the را marker makes it definite/emphatic. چو = “when.” روی ... به روی is a poetic duplication meaning “face to face / opposite one another”; اندرآمد is past “came/entered.” The word order is somewhat inverted for verse: topic (سپه را) then temporal clause then verb phrase indicating arrival “to face.”
segments.s574-g30: بی‌آرام شد is a two-word predicate meaning “became restless” (بی‌آرام = restless, شد = became). مردم کینه‌جوی is the subject phrase “the vengeful people” (مردم = people, کینه‌جوی = revenge-seeking/vengeful); the subject follows the predicate here for stylistic emphasis, which is acceptable in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s574-g31: رده برکشیدند: رده “rank/row” + برکشیدند (past 3rd‑person plural “they raised/drew up”), meaning they formed or lifted their ranks. و is “and.” برخاست غو literally places the verb برخاست (“arose”) before غو (“uproar”); this inversion (verb then noun) is poetic and means “an uproar arose.”
segments.s574-g32: بیامد is past 3rd‑person singular “came.” دوان is an adverbial participle/gerund meaning “running” or “in haste” and modifies the manner of coming. یانس is the subject (proper name). پیش رو is a locative/phrase meaning “to the front / ahead” (پیش = before/in front, رو = face/toward), so the whole phrase means “Yans came running to the front.”
segments.s574-g1: This nominal phrase (عرض‌گاه و دیوان) appears before the verb, typical in Persian; the past-tense verb بیاراستند (bi-ārāstand) is 3rd-person plural 'they adorned/dressed'. The subject is omitted and understood from context. دیوان here is an institutional/place word (the royal diwan/court), not a poetic collection.
segments.s574-g2: کلید در گنجها is the object phrase placed before the verb خواستند (3rd-person plural past 'they wanted/sought'). در here is the noun 'door/gate' (i.e. 'keys of the doors of the treasuries'), and گنجها is literal 'treasures/treasuries'. خواستند functions as a simple past 'they sought/wanted'.
segments.s574-g3: سپاه انجمن شد is 'the army became/formed an assembly' with شد as the past copula/verb 'became'. چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; روزی بداد is a poetic phrase 'a day of بداد (oppression) appeared' — روزی is used as 'a day/when it dawned' and بداد functions as a noun 'oppression' or ill event.
segments.s574-g4: سرش and دلش are possessive constructions (سر + enclitic -ش, دل + -ش) meaning 'his head' and 'his heart'. پر ز (full of) is the adjectival phrase where ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. The two coordinated predicates describe his inner state: سرش پر ز کین 'his head full of hatred' and دلش پر ز باد 'his heart full of wind' (باد here literal 'wind', used metaphorically).
segments.s574-g5: از ایران همی راند: از marks origin 'from Iran'; همی is the continuous/iterative marker (in epic Persian it gives a sense of repeated or continuous past action: 'kept on/was driving'); راند is past 'drove/expelled'. تا here is the extent marker meaning 'as far as' (to the border of Rome), so تا مرز روم = 'up to the Roman border'.
segments.s574-g6: هرانکس که بود is an archaic fused relative 'whoever was'; که introduces the relative clause and بود is the past copula 'was'. اندران (اندر + ان) is a locative 'in/among them' or 'in that place' modifying مرز و بوم 'border and native soil'. The whole clause means 'whosoever was in that border and land'.
segments.s574-g7: بکشتند is 3rd-person plural past 'they killed'. خانش is Khan + enclitic -ش 'its/their khan(s)/inn(s)' — here خان is a built structure ('inn, caravanserai') rather than an honorific title — and همی سوختند uses همی for repeated/continuous past 'kept burning' with سوختند 'they burned/it burned'.
segments.s574-g8: جهانی is 'the whole world' (universal/total), here the direct object. به آتش brafrokhtand is the verbal phrase 'they kindled/set on fire' where به marks direction/instrument ('into/with fire') and برافروختند is past 3rd plural 'they kindled/ignited'.
segments.s574-g65: This clause is an optative subordinate introduced by که ('that/that it be so'), closing with the negative wish مباد ('may it not be'). ما را + را marks the experiencer/possessor ('for us' / 'among us'). چنو نیز مهتر is a noun phrase where چنو ('such') modifies مهتر ('chief, headman'); نیز ('also') adds the sense 'too/also'. The overall sense: 'that among us there be not also such a chief.'
segments.s574-g66: This is a short locative wish clause with مباد ('may it not be') final. به + اندرون gives the direction/location 'in/within' (به ... اندرون = 'in the inner part of'). نام قیصر is the noun phrase 'the name/title Caesar'; here نام means the literal name/title. The clause means 'may the name Caesar not be in the interior of Rome.'
segments.s574-g67: به روم اندرون is a locative phrase 'in the inner part of Rome.' جای مذبح is a noun phrase 'place for the altar' (جای = 'place / room for', مذبح = 'altar'). The verb نماند is a negative existential/past sense 'did not remain / there was none left', so the clause reads 'there remained no place for an altar in Rome's interior.'
segments.s574-g68: This is a simple nominal list followed by the same negative existential verb نماند: 'صلیب و مسیح و موشح' enumerates objects ('the cross, Christ, and ornament/images'), and نماند states they 'were not left' / 'remained not'. The coordinating و joins the items; موشح here means decorative/ornamental items or decorated images rather than the poetic sense.
segments.s574-g69: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. زنار قسیس is a genitive-like construction 'the priest's girdle' (زنار = girdle, قسیس = priest). شد is the past/become auxiliary 'became/was', and سوخته is a predicative past participle 'burnt.' Together: 'when the priest's girdle became burnt' = 'when the priest's girdle was burnt.'
segments.s574-g70: This coordinate noun phrase names two ecclesiastical items/figures: چلیپا ('cross') and مطران ('metropolitan/bishop'). برافروخته is an adjectival/predicative form meaning 'kindled/ignited' or 'set aflame' (literally 'made bright/ablaze'). Classical Persian often places the predicate after the list without an explicit auxiliary: 'the cross and the bishop were kindled.'
segments.s574-g71: کنون ('now') opens the clause. روم و قنوج are two place names coordinated by و. ما را uses را as an experiencer/ dative-like marker ('to us' / 'for us'), and یکیست is a nominal predicate 'is one / is the same.' The construction means 'Now Rome and Kannauj are one to us' = 'are the same for us.'
segments.s574-g72: چو here introduces a circumstantial/casual clause 'since/as' (comparative/temporal). آواز دین مسیح is a genitive chain 'the voice/call of the religion of Christ' (آواز = voice/call, دین = religion, مسیح = Christ). اندکیست is an existential/adjectival predicate 'is little / is slight,' giving the reason or circumstance: 'since the voice of Christ's religion is slight.'
segments.s574-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آگاهی آمد is a past-tense clause 'news/information came' with آمد as the simple past 3rd‑singular verb. ز ایران به روم places source then destination (ز = 'from', به = 'to'), so the segment reads literally 'when information came from Iran to Rome.'
segments.s574-g10: که introduces a reported-content subordinate clause ('that ...'). ویران شد is a past inchoative/passive sense 'was ruined/laid waste' with شد as the past auxiliary; آن مرز آباد بوم is a poetic nominal phrase ('that prosperous border‑land/homeland') in which آباد modifies the locale (مرز/بوم), giving 'that prosperous frontier homeland.'
segments.s574-g11: گرفتار شد is a passive/inchoative phrase meaning 'was captured/was taken' (شد marks the past); the subject follows the verb in poetic inversion: قیصر نامدار = 'the famed Caesar', where نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned.'
segments.s574-g12: شب تیره is a temporal adverbial 'the dark night'; اندر (archaic/prepositional) = 'in/within' and introduces the locative phrase صف کارزار 'the battle line/ranks' (صف here = battle formation). Word order places the time ('شب تیره') before the location ('اندر صف کارزار').
segments.s574-g13: سراسر همه روم is an emphatic subject phrase 'all throughout Rome' (سراسر = 'throughout', همه روم = 'all Rome/everyone in Rome'), followed by the predicative adjective گریان plus the past auxiliary شدند: 'became weeping' = 'wept.'
segments.s574-g14: وز here functions as an archaic/poetic preposition equivalent to 'از' ('by/at/from the sound of'); آواز شاپور is a genitive phrase 'Shapur's voice/cry'; بریان شدند is a metaphoric predicative use ('were consumed/tormented') with شدند as the auxiliary. (Because the listed senses for وز do not include 'from', its sense is marked separately below.)
segments.s574-g15: همی گفت is an iterative past construction 'kept saying' (همی gives the continuous/iterative flavour); هرکس که این بد که کرد is an exclamatory/relative clause 'whoever/whomever did this bad deed' (the doubled که is an archaic/poetic repetition linking the relative/question to the verb کرد 'did').
segments.s574-g16: مگر introduces an exception/rhetorical 'except/other than' (here implying 'who else could it be but...'); قیصر آن ناجوانمرد مرد places the name and an emphatic descriptive phrase 'that dishonourable man' (ناجوانمرد modifying مرد), identifying Caesar as the presumed culprit.
segments.s574-g57: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'. 'هر جای' ('each place') is a compound noun phrase with the determiner هر preceding جای. 'چندان' is an adverb meaning 'so much / to such an extent' modifying the noun غنیمت, and گرفت is the past tense (3rd person singular) of گرفتن 'to seize/take', completing the verb phrase 'he seized so much booty'. Word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase → quantified NP → verb.
segments.s574-g58: که introduces a result clause ('so that / such that'). لشکر is the subject 'the army'. همی is a poetic/progressive adverb here ('still/kept on' / emphatic), and ماند is the past-tense verb 'remained' (3rd sing.). زو is the contracted poetic preposition+pronoun 'from/of it' referring back to the booty; در شگفت is a locative phrase 'in astonishment' describing the state in which the army remained.
segments.s574-g59: ببخشید is the past tense 3rd-person-plural (they) of بخشیدن in the sense 'to give/bestow' here (poetic narrative past). یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/at once' that modifies the verb phrase; همه is the direct object 'everything/all'. بر سپاه is the prepositional phrase 'to/upon the army' marking the beneficiary.
segments.s574-g60: This clause expresses the exception: جز از گنج قیصر = 'except from Caesar's treasure'. نبد is an archaic/poetic negative copula meaning 'there was not' or 'did not exist'. بهر شاه means 'for the king' (بهر = for/toward, شاه = king). The whole clause thus says nothing of Caesar's treasure remained for the king.
segments.s574-g61: کجا is a rhetorical 'where' (introducing a negative/ rhetorical question: 'where/when would it be that...'). دیده‌بد is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'the envious / ill‑eyed' used as 'an envious person'. رنج از گنج اوی = 'suffering from his treasure' where از marks source/causal relation and اوی is the old poetic possessive 'his'. The verb 'has seen' is not explicit; the clause is elliptical/rhetorical: 'Where is there an envious one who suffers from his treasure?'
segments.s574-g62: نه هم is 'nor even' (negation carried over from the preceding rhetorical sense). گوشه 'a corner/part' of the treasure is the noun, بد qualifies it as 'bad/blemished', and گنج is 'treasure'. با رنج اوی = 'by/with his hardship' (با = with, رنج = hardship, اوی = his). The clause says that no part of the treasure is tainted or made bad by his hardships.
segments.s574-g63: همه modifies لشکر to mean 'the entire host/army'. روم specifies which army (Rome/Byzantium). گرد آمدند is a verbal compound meaning 'gathered about / came together' (گرد here 'around/about', آمدند = past plural 'they came'). The order is subject (modified) → locative/adverbial → verb as is common.
segments.s574-g64: ز قیصر is a poetic 'of/from Caesar' (here 'about Caesar' or 'concerning Caesar'). همی is the narrative/progressive adverb (here giving the sense 'kept on / were continually'), and داستانها زدند uses زدند ('to strike/sound') in a literary sense 'to tell/recite' (they uttered/told tales). The clause thus means they kept telling/relating tales about Caesar.
segments.s574-g41: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning "when." از قلبِ شاپور is a genitive prepositional phrase "from Shapur's heart/core." لشکر is the noun (the host/army) and براند is a third-person past/ narrative form of راندن used here as a finite verb meaning "sent/drave forth" (classical/persian epic word order places the verb before or next to the subject). The clause as a whole: "When Shapur sent the army forth from his heart."
segments.s574-g42: چپ و راستش literally "his left and (his) right" (چپ = left; راستش here denotes the right side with the possessive -ش). ویژگان را is the definite/direct object marked by را: ویژگان = "the distinguished (men)" and را marks them as the object. بخواند is a third-person past/narrative form of خواندن used here with the sense "to call/summon." The clause means: "he called/summoned the distinguished ones to his left and right."
segments.s574-g43: چو = "when." با مهتران = "with/among the chiefs" (مهتران = chiefs/elders; an archaic plural). گرم کرد is a compound where گرم (here "roused/spirited/zealous") + کرد (make/do) means "made (it) spirited/roused"; اسپ is the direct object "the steed/horse," and شاه at the end is the subject "the king" (epic inversion placing the subject after the verb for emphasis). Together: "When the king made his steed spirited among the chiefs."
segments.s574-g44: زمین گشت combines زمین (the earth/ground) with the verb گشت "became/was turned," so "the earth became (something)." جنبـان and پیچان are predicative adjectives/participial forms meaning "moving" and "winding/twisting." سپاه at the end is the noun being described too ("and the army [was] twisting"); the verb گشت governs the predicative descriptions. Poetic inversion places modifiers before or after the noun for effect: overall, "the earth became moving, and the army twisting and turning."
segments.s574-g45: سوی لشکر رومیان is a directional phrase "toward the Roman army." حمله is the noun "attack/assault" and برد is the light verb attached to حمله (classic compound meaning "carried/led an attack," i.e. "launched an attack"). Word order places the direction first and then the verb phrase: "(he) launched an attack toward the Roman host."
segments.s574-g46: بزرگش = بزرگ + possessive suffix -ش "his great (one)/one of his great men." یکی بود = "there was one" (یکی = one; بود = past of 'to be'). با here links a noun phrase in a predicative/appositive sense: باِ مردِ خرد = "a man of wisdom" (مرد خرد = wise man). So the phrase says: "one of his great men was a man of wisdom."
segments.s574-g47: بدانست is the past tense "he knew/understood." The sequence has the verb before the proper name (بدانست یانس), a common epic inversion: "Yāns understood/realized." که introduces the content clause "that ..." and پایاب شاه is the embedded noun phrase (پایاب here a technical/archaic word referring to an outlet or irrigated water-supply connected to qanats/fields). The clause begins the reported content: "that the king's پایاب ..."
segments.s574-g48: ندارد is the negative-present/presentive form "does not have" (in narrative functioning as "had no"); it completes the content clause: "that the king has/ had no پایاب." گریزان بشد is a periphrastic expression: گریزان (fleeing) + بشد (became/it happened) = "he took to flight/ he fled." با سپاه = "with the army" (با = with; سپاه = army) marks accompaniment. So: "(he) fled with his army."
segments.s574-g49: پس‌اندر is an adverb meaning 'afterwards' that sets the sequence. همی before a past-tense verb (تاخت) is the archaic continuous/progressive marker (like 'kept/was ...ing'). تاخت is the past/preterite of 'to gallop/charge'. شاپور is the subject name. گرد here is a noun 'dust' that will link to the next phrase ('dust ...').
segments.s574-g50: به is the locative/prepositional marker introducing the effect ('(so) by/with'). گرد is the same noun 'dust' resumed from the previous segment. از + هوا = 'from the air/sky' (از = from, هوا = air/atmosphere). روشنایی is the direct object 'brightness/light'. ببرد is a classical third-person verb form (here narrative past sense) meaning 'took/carried away' (ب- prefix appears in older narrative forms); it completes the clause describing what the dust did.
segments.s574-g51: به هر جایگه is a locative phrase 'at every place/spot'. بر یکی literally 'upon one' — here 'one' qualifies the heap placed on each spot. توده 'heap/stack' is the object and کرد is the past-tense verb 'made/placed' (کرد used as causative/performative 'he made a heap'). Word order: locative → object → verb.
segments.s574-g52: گیاها is the plural/collective 'plants/vegetation' (the material used). به مغز سر is a locative phrase 'on the crown/top of the head' (مغز here = 'core/top' of سر). آلوده is an adjectival/predicative participle 'dirty/soiled', and کرد (again) is the verb 'made' — together 'made (their) crowns of the head dirty with plants'.
segments.s574-g53: ازان لشکر روم = 'of that army/host of Rome' (ازان = 'of that/from that'). چندان is an adverb/quantifier 'so many/to that extent'. بکشت is an archaic narrative perfect/past 'he killed' (the b‑prefix is used in older poetic narration); together the clause means 'he killed so many of the Roman host'.
segments.s574-g54: که introduces a result/consequence clause 'so that'. یک دشت = 'a plain' or 'a whole plain'. سر here is plural/collective 'heads' (anatomical sense). بود is the past copula 'was'. بی‌پای is an adjective 'without feet/footless' and پشت 'backs' completes the description ('without feet and backs'). Grammar: relative/result clause describing the aftermath of the killing.
segments.s574-g55: به هامون is locative 'on the plain'. سپاه و چلیپا نماند = 'there remained neither army nor cross' — نماند is the negative perfective/preterite 'did not remain / there was none left'. Word order places the locative first for emphasis, then the coordinated items, then the verb.
segments.s574-g56: به دژها 'in/at the fortresses' is the locative. صلیب و سکوبا نماند repeats the same structure: 'no cross and no سکوبا remained.' سکوبا is an obscure/rare term in the lexicon (the line lists what was gone from the fortresses). نماند again negates existence ('was not left').
segments.s574-g17: ز + قیصر = a poetic/concise way to say “one of Caesar’s” (ز = “from/of”). یکی is an indefinite 'one' (a certain person) and که introduces a relative clause 'who…'. برادرش combines برادر + the enclitic ش ('his'), and بود is the past copula 'was' linking the relative clause to 'one'.
segments.s574-g18: This is a simple nominal predicate: پدر مرده و زنده مادرش بود = 'his father was dead and his mother was alive.' مرده and زنده are predicate adjectives; ش is the possessive clitic referring back to 'one'; بود is the past copula applying to both coordinated predicates.
segments.s574-g19: جوانی is a temporal noun 'in youth' (setting the time). کجا here is adverbial/intensifying in older style but functions with the following clause; یانسش = یانس + possessive ش 'his name Yans'; بود is the past copula; نام ('name') is placed after the clause and links to the following epithets.
segments.s574-g20: A string of nominal epithets describing him (appositive to نام): جهانجوی 'world-seeker' (a compound noun), سپس conjunctions و linking adjectives بخشنده 'generous' and شادکام 'fortunate'. The sequence is a nominal predicate/apposition to the preceding 'his name'.
segments.s574-g21: شدند (past plural) + انجمن لشکری = 'an assembly/troop of soldiers formed' or 'soldiers assembled'; بر followed by درش = 'upon/at his gate' (در here = 'gate/door', ش = possessive clitic 'his'). The word order puts the assembled host before the location phrase.
segments.s574-g22: درم داد is the verb phrase 'gave dirhams' (درم = dirham, داد = gave). The agent 'پرخاشجو مادرش' ('his quarrelsome mother') follows the verb, a common poetic word order where the object/verb precede the agent phrase; ش on مادر indicates 'his'.
segments.s574-g23: بدو is the clitic + preposition 'to him' (i.e. 'he said to him'). گفت is the past tense 'said' (here introducing reported speech). کین برادر بخواه is an imperative/subjunctive clause: کین 'vengeance' (object), برادر 'brother', and بخواه is the imperative/optative 'seek/ask for' — together: 'seek vengeance on (your) brother.'
segments.s574-g24: نبینی is a negative prohibitive/subjunctive 'do not (let it be that) you see/wait' — a warning not to delay; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that an army has come', آمد is the past 'came', ز ایران سپاه = 'an army from Iran' with ز = 'from' and سپاه 'army' as the head noun. The whole clause: 'do not wait until an army comes from Iran.'
segments.s574-g33: برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'rose' placed before its phrase (typical for epic/poetic word order). یکی ابر و گردی سیاه is the subject phrase: یکی = 'a certain / a' (indefinite determiner) modifies ابر 'cloud', و links گردی 'a little dust/whirl' as a coordinated noun, and سیاه is a postpositive adjective 'dark/gloomy' modifying گردی. The construction is literal: 'A certain cloud and a dark dust-swirl rose.'
segments.s574-g34: کزان is a compound prepositional/relative particle 'from which', linking back to the risen cloud/dust. تیرگی is the noun 'darkness/gloom' that results; دیده stands for 'the eye' or 'sight' (the affected entity). گم کرد is a causative/lexical compound here meaning 'caused to lose' / 'lost' (کرد = 'caused/made'), and راه is the thing lost 'the way' or visibility. Word order is somewhat inverted: 'From which darkness sight made lose the way' → 'from which the darkness caused sight to lose the way.'
segments.s574-g35: سپه را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker placing 'the army' as the topic. به یک روی means 'on one side' (یک quantifies روی 'side/face'), and بر کوه بود is the locative predicate 'was on the mountain' with بود as past 'was'. The overall order is topical noun + locative phrase + copula 'was'.
segments.s574-g36: دگر begins a contrasting clause 'on the other side'. آب زانسو is a genitival phrase 'the water (river) Zansu' where زانسو is a proper name; که introduces a relative clause 'which', انبوه is the adjective 'crowded/dense', and بود is the copula 'was'. So: 'on the other side the Zansu waters, which were dense, (were).'
segments.s574-g37: بدین گونه is a fixed adverbial phrase 'in this manner' (بدین = 'in this' formed from the prefix بـ + an old/deictic), تا is the subordinating conjunction 'until', گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned', and خورشید زرد is the noun + adjective 'the sun (became) yellow/pale' serving as the clause's complement. The clause means 'in this way, until the sun became yellow.'
segments.s574-g38: ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction' (ز = 'from', هر سو = 'every side'), همی خاست is the imperfective/past narrative 'rose up' or 'kept rising' (همی = continuous/epic present marker, خاست = 'arose'), and گرد نبرد is a nominal phrase 'the dust of battle' where نبرد ('battle') qualifies گرد ('dust'). The sequence describes the rising battle-dust from all directions.
segments.s574-g39: بکشتند is the past plural verb 'they killed/slaughtered'. چندانک is a conjunctive adverb 'to such an extent / so that' introducing the result clause. روی زمین is the noun phrase 'the surface/face of the earth' (روی = 'surface/face', زمین = 'earth/ground'). The clause sets up a result: 'they killed so much that...'.
segments.s574-g40: شد is the resultative copula 'became' (the ground became ...). از introduces the material/source phrase 'from/out of', جوشن کشتگان آهنین is a nested noun phrase: جوشن 'cuirass/armor', کشتگان 'of the slain' (the slain ones), and آهنین postpositively 'iron' describing the armor. Together: 'the ground became (covered) with the iron cuirasses of the slain.'

sentences.s574-s13: When Yans heard it, he seethed and said that a brother's hatred must not be concealed.
sentences.s574-s14: He struck the war-drum and brought out a great cross and a fearsome army.
sentences.s574-s15: When the army came face to face, the vengeful people became restless.
sentences.s574-s16: They drew up their ranks and an uproar arose; Yans came running to the front.
sentences.s574-s1: They dressed the audience-hall and the diwan, and sought the keys of the treasure-doors.
sentences.s574-s2: The army formed an assembly when a day of oppression appeared; his head was full of hatred and his heart full of wind.
sentences.s574-s3: They drove them out of Iran as far as the border of Rome; whoever was in that border and homeland (was driven out).
sentences.s574-s4: They killed (them) and burned their khans/inns; they set the whole world ablaze with fire.
sentences.s574-s33: May we not have such a chief among us, and may the name 'Caesar' not be in the inner parts of Rome.
sentences.s574-s34: In the interior of Rome there remained no place for an altar; no cross, no Christ, no ornament remained.
sentences.s574-s35: When the priest's girdle was burnt, the cross and the metropolitan/bishop were set afire.
sentences.s574-s36: Now Rome and Kannauj are the same to us, since the voice of Christ's religion is slight.
sentences.s574-s5: When news came to Rome from Iran that that prosperous frontier homeland had been laid waste.
sentences.s574-s6: The renowned Caesar was captured during a dark night among the ranks of battle.
sentences.s574-s7: All through Rome people wept, and at Shapur's cry they were consumed (with anguish).
sentences.s574-s8: Everyone kept saying, 'Whoever did this evil — who else but Caesar, that dishonourable man?'.
sentences.s574-s29: From every place he seized so much booty that the army remained amazed at it.
sentences.s574-s30: They gave everything outright to the army; of Caesar's treasure there was nothing for the king.
sentences.s574-s31: Where has an envious eye suffered from his treasure? Nor is any corner of the treasure ill with his hardship.
sentences.s574-s32: The whole Roman host gathered together, and they went on telling tales of Caesar.
sentences.s574-s21: When Shapur sent his host forth from his heart, he summoned the distinguished men to his left and his right.
sentences.s574-s22: When the king roused his steed among the chiefs, the earth was set in motion and the army twisted and turned.
sentences.s574-s23: He made an attack toward the Roman army; one of his great men was a man of wisdom.
sentences.s574-s24: Yāns understood that the king had no outlet/water-supply, and he fled with his army.
sentences.s574-s25: Then Shapur kept charging, and the dust he raised stole the brightness from the air.
sentences.s574-s26: At every spot he heaped up a bundle, he covered their heads with vegetation and made them dirty.
sentences.s574-s27: He killed so many of that Roman host that the plain was full of heads, without feet or backs.
sentences.s574-s28: On the plain there remained neither army nor cross; in the fortresses there remained neither cross nor skouba.
sentences.s574-s9: There was one of Caesar's — his brother — whose father was dead and whose mother was alive.
sentences.s574-s10: In his youth his name was Yans — a seeker of the world, generous and fortunate.
sentences.s574-s11: A band of soldiers assembled at his gate; his quarrelsome mother gave dirhams.
sentences.s574-s12: He said to him, 'Seek vengeance on your brother; do not wait until an army comes from Iran.'
sentences.s574-s17: A cloud and a dark swirl of dust rose up, from which the darkness made sight lose its way.
sentences.s574-s18: On one side the army was on the mountain; on the other side flowed the waters of Zansu, which were dense.
sentences.s574-s19: Thus, until the sun grew yellow, from every side there rose the dust of battle.
sentences.s574-s20: They slew so many that the surface of the ground became covered with the iron cuirasses of the slain.
